metadata.summary: Service layer pattern separates business logic into dedicated functions with keyword-only args, typed signatures, and explicit transaction boundaries.

content.title: Django Services Layer
content.summary: The service layer pattern separates business logic from Django models, views, and serializers into dedicated functions with keyword-only arguments, typed signatures, and explicit transaction boundaries. Services own write operations (CREATE/UPDATE/DELETE and external API calls); selectors handle complex read queries. The canonical naming convention is entity_action (order_create, user_deactivate). Every service that performs two or more writes must use @transaction.atomic.

why.p: Fat views and fat models accumulate untestable, unreusable logic. The service layer creates a boundary where tests inject mock dependencies, multiple entry points (API, admin, CLI) call the same function, and transaction safety is explicit rather than implicit. The HackSoft Django Styleguide's empirical benchmark: services reduce view line-count by approximately 70 percent while enabling 90 percent plus branch coverage on business rules.

list.item: Any CREATE/UPDATE/DELETE that touches two or more models or invokes an external API.
Replacing fat-model methods that do email plus payment plus audit log in one blob.
Building a testable surface for business rules with no Django request/response in tests.
Layering DRF: serializers validate input, views delegate to services, services own writes.
Simple property calculations — use @property on the model.
Pure querysets / chained filters — use a custom manager.
Read-only, permission-aware fetches — use a selector, not a service.
Trivial single-model CRUD where a direct ORM call is clearer.

section.name: naming
section.p: Name service functions entity_action to group all operations on a domain object together, making grep and impact analysis reliable.

rule.statement: Name service functions entity_action (e.g. order_create, user_deactivate). Choose one convention project-wide and document it in the project AGENTS.md.
rule.rationale: entity_action groups all operations on a domain object together, making grep -r "order_" reliable for impact analysis.

section.name: signature
section.p: All service function arguments must be keyword-only with full type hints. Use TYPE_CHECKING imports for model types to avoid circular imports at runtime.

rule.statement: All service function arguments must be keyword-only (leading * separator). Include full type hints on all parameters and the return type. Use TYPE_CHECKING imports for model types.
rule.rationale: Keyword-only prevents positional mistakes when signatures grow. TYPE_CHECKING avoids circular imports at runtime while keeping type checkers happy.

example.title: Properly typed service signature
example.code:
from __future__ import annotations from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING: from apps.orders.models import Order from apps.users.models import User
def order_create( *, user: User, items: list[dict], notes: str | None = None, ) -> Order: ...

example.title: Positional arguments with missing types
example.code: def order_create(user, items, notes=None): # positional, no types ...
example.note: Positional args cause silent mistakes; missing types block pyright from catching signature drift.

section.name: transactions
section.p: Apply @transaction.atomic only when the service performs two or more writes that must succeed together. For side effects that must not run on rollback, use transaction.on_commit() or task.delay_on_commit().

rule.statement: Apply @transaction.atomic only when the service performs two or more writes that must succeed together. Do not wrap read-only services or single-write operations.
rule.rationale: Unnecessary atomic blocks hold DB locks longer and nest savepoints with surprising rollback semantics.

rule.statement: For any side effect that must not run on rollback (Celery tasks, webhook fires, email), use transaction.on_commit() or task.delay_on_commit().
rule.rationale: task.delay() inside an atomic block can execute before the commit completes, causing the task to query a row that does not yet exist.

example.title: Service with atomic block and delayed task

section.name: input-validation
section.p: Validate business rules at the service entry point with custom exceptions. Never let views validate business rules, so all entry points (API, admin, CLI) enforce the same rules.

rule.statement: Validate business rules at the service entry point. Raise a custom ValidationError with a descriptive message. Never let views validate business rules.
rule.rationale: Centralised validation means admin, API, and CLI entry points all enforce the same rules.

example.title: Business rule validation in service

section.name: file-organisation
section.p: For small apps use a single services.py file. For larger apps create a services/ package with one module per entity (user_services.py, order_services.py) and re-export the public API in services/__init__.py.

rule.statement: For apps with fewer than 10 services use a single services.py. For larger apps, create a services/ package with one module per bounded entity (user_services.py, order_services.py) and re-export the public API in services/__init__.py.
rule.rationale: Re-exporting from __init__.py keeps call-sites stable when modules are split later.

section.name: error-handling
section.p: Define a service exception hierarchy rooted at ServiceError with domain-specific subclasses. Let exceptions propagate rather than swallowing them, so the caller (view, task) can map exceptions to HTTP status codes or retry decisions.

rule.statement: Define a service exception hierarchy rooted at ServiceError. Use domain-specific subclasses (InsufficientStockError, PaymentFailedError). Let exceptions propagate; do not swallow them inside services.
rule.rationale: Caller (view, task) maps exceptions to HTTP status codes or retry decisions. Swallowing silences bugs.

section.name: selectors-optimised-reads
section.p: The selector pattern isolates complex read logic into dedicated functions, preventing N+1 queries and keeping views and services thin.

rule.statement: Create a selector function (not a service) when: the query spans multiple relations, includes permission-based filtering, involves aggregations, or is reused across multiple views.
rule.rationale: Selectors return QuerySets for further chaining. Placing read logic in a selector makes it testable in isolation and reusable from any entry point.

section.name: n-plus-one-prevention
section.p: Every selector must document its expected query count in the docstring. Use select_related() for FK/OneToOne and prefetch_related() for reverse FK/M2M. Add only() or defer() to limit loaded columns when serialising large sets.

rule.statement: Every selector must document its expected query count in the docstring. Use select_related() for FK/OneToOne and prefetch_related() for reverse FK/M2M. Add only() or defer() to limit loaded columns when serialising large sets.
rule.rationale: Unoptimised querysets serialised in DRF loop over relations, issuing one query per row (N+1). Prefetch collapses this to 2 queries.

example.title: Optimised selector with prefetch
example.code:
from django.db.models import Count, QuerySet
def order_list_for_user(*, user: User) -> QuerySet[Order]: """ Get visible orders for a user with items prefetched.
Query count: 3 (orders, items, products) """ return ( Order.objects .filter(user=user, is_visible=True) .select_related("user", "payment") .prefetch_related("items__product") .annotate(item_count=Count("items")) .order_by("-created_at") )

example.title: Unoptimised selector causing N+1
example.code: def order_list_for_user(*, user: User): return Order.objects.filter(user=user) # N+1 when serialised
example.note: Missing prefetch causes one extra query per order when serialiser accesses items.

section.name: aggregation-selectors
section.p: Aggregation selectors must return a QuerySet so callers can chain additional filters. Document the output shape in the docstring.

rule.statement: Aggregation selectors must return a QuerySet, not a plain dict, so callers can chain additional filters. Document the output shape in the docstring.
rule.rationale: Returning a QuerySet keeps the selector composable; a plain dict forces a new selector every time a caller needs slightly different filters.

example.title: Composable aggregation selector
example.code:
from datetime import date from django.db.models import Avg, Count, Sum, QuerySet from django.db.models.functions import TruncMonth
def sales_monthly_summary(*, start_date: date, end_date: date) -> QuerySet: """ Monthly sales summary.
Query count: 1 Output columns: month, total_sales, order_count, avg_order_value """ from apps.orders.models import Order
return ( Order.objects .filter( status="completed", created_at__date__gte=start_date, created_at__date__lte=end_date, ) .annotate(month=TruncMonth("created_at")) .values("month") .annotate( total_sales=Sum("total"), order_count=Count("id"), avg_order_value=Avg("total"), ) .order_by("month") )

section.name: patterns-and-examples
section.p: Concrete examples for common migration patterns and architecture decisions.

rule.statement: Inject external services (payment gateways, email senders) as Protocol parameters. Never import SDK clients at module level inside a service.
rule.rationale: Protocol parameters let tests pass Mock() objects without patching module globals, which is fragile.

example.title: Business logic in service with thin view
Protocol-based dependency injection
example.code:
from typing import Protocol
class PaymentGateway(Protocol): def charge(self, amount: int, customer_id: str) -> str: ... def refund(self, transaction_id: str) -> bool: ...
def order_process_payment( *, order: Order, payment_gateway: PaymentGateway, ) -> Order: transaction_id = payment_gateway.charge( amount=int(order.total * 100), customer_id=order.user.payment_customer_id, ) order.payment_transaction_id = transaction_id order.status = "paid" order.save(update_fields=["payment_transaction_id", "status", "updated_at"]) return order

section.name: agent-gotchas
section.p: Most frequent mistakes when generating service code.

antipattern.description: LLMs default to positional arguments and skip the leading * separator.
antipattern.reason: Enforce keyword-only via ruff RET rule or a pyright reportMissingTypeArgument check in CI.

antipattern.description: Agents call services from inside DRF ModelSerializer.create() because tutorials show it.
antipattern.reason: Correct pattern: view calls service, serialiser handles I/O only. Serialiser business logic is untestable without HTTP.

antipattern.description: Agents stack @transaction.atomic on both the outer service and inner helper services, creating savepoints with non-obvious rollback semantics.
antipattern.reason: Only the outermost service in a flow should be atomic. Document the rule in the project AGENTS.md.

antipattern.description: Agents mock ORM methods (Order.objects.create) to avoid a real DB in tests.
antipattern.reason: Use real DB plus factory_boy. Mocking ORM makes tests pass while hiding query issues.

template.purpose: Canonical service function skeleton with docstring, TYPE_CHECKING, @transaction.atomic.
Selector function skeleton with prefetch, query-count docstring.
Service exception hierarchy: ServiceError, ValidationError, NotFoundError, BusinessRuleError, ExternalServiceError.
Factory Boy factories for User, Order, Product, OrderItem.
LLM prompt template for generating a complete service.
